title: Chapter 46: The Extraction
content: They moved through Blackmoss like a virus through a host.

Wren carried Ash's weight on her right side, his arm hooked over her shoulder, his feet dragging more than walking. The suppressant was still in his system — she could hear it in the irregular rhythm of his glitch, a heartbeat skipping and stuttering like a damaged engine. But he was fighting it. Every step he took was a battle against the drug, and he was winning, slowly, one agonizing foot at a time.

She navigated by data stream. The facility's digital architecture sang around her — security protocols, camera feeds, door schedules, environmental controls. She reached into each system as they passed, leaving a trail of confusion in their wake.
Camera ahead: fuzzed. Twelve seconds of static before the auto-correction kicked in.
Locked door: override. The magnetic seal released with a thunk that echoed down the corridor.
Hound patrol approaching from the west: she pulled them into a service closet and waited, Ash's breathing ragged against her neck, while the mechanical feet clanked past.

She was getting faster. The glitch moved through her like adrenaline now, instinct instead of effort. She didn't have to think about fuzzing a camera any more than she had to think about breathing. She just did it.
"Left," Ash murmured. His voice was stronger than it had been in the cell — rough, but coherent. "Server room is left."

She turned left. The corridor here was wider, the floor done in anti-static tile instead of concrete. The hum of the data streams was louder — a deep, resonant chord that vibrated in her chest. They were close.
"Second door," Ash said. "Big one. Reinforced."

She saw it. A steel door with a biometric lock rated higher than the service entrance — Class 5, multiple verification layers, hardened against digital intrusion. The kind of door designed to keep people out of something important.
Or keep something in.

She pressed her palm to the reader and pushed. The lock resisted — not a simple frequency to mimic this time, but a layered authentication protocol that checked itself three times before granting access. She felt sweat break out on her forehead. The glitch strained against the complexity. Her nose started bleeding again.
The lock clicked.
She shouldered the door open and half-dragged Ash inside.

The server room was enormous. Racks of equipment stretched in rows like trees in a digital forest, blinking with status lights, thrumming with the deep bass note of data in motion. The air was cool and dry and tasted of ozone. The floor was raised, industrial grating over a tangle of cables thick as her wrist. And at the far end, connected to the biggest rack in the room, was the core server — a monolith of black metal and glass, its surface alive with scrolling data.
This was it. The heart of Blackmoss. The central node that connected the facility to the outside world.
Ash collapsed against the nearest rack. His legs gave out and he slid down until he was sitting on the floor, back against the cold metal. His head lolled.
"I can't—" He shook his head. "The suppressant. It's still—"

"You don't have to." Wren knelt beside him, grabbed his face, made him look at her. His gaze was glassy but focusing. The tattoos on his arms were pulsing now — not bright, but brighter. "You don't have to do anything. Just stay alive. That's all I need from you."
"That's a lot," he said. And despite everything — the drugs, the captivity, the impossible situation — he almost smiled.

She pulled the Signal Key from her jacket. It pulsed in her hand, recognizing the proximity to the core server — a vibration that climbed from her palm to her elbow, a frequency that made her teeth ache. She found the access port on the server's front panel. A universal interface, industry standard — NeuroScale's arrogance, assuming no one would ever get this far.
She plugged in the Key.

The effect was immediate. Every screen in the server room flickered. The status lights on the racks shifted from green to amber. The hum of the data streams changed pitch — higher, sharper, a tuning fork struck and resonating.

She was connected. The Key was bridging her glitch-power to the core server's broadcast capabilities. She could feel the network opening up around her like a flower — the facility's internal systems, the external relay, the fiber lines that connected Blackmoss to the city's telecommunications grid.
She started pushing.
And immediately hit a wall.

Ash. His system was interfering. The suppressant still coursing through his blood created a dead zone around him — a bubble of signal degradation that crackled and popped in the data stream. She could feel it, a harsh dissonance in the network's music, and every time she tried to push the broadcast signal past him, it distorted.

She needed stability. She needed to hold the Key *and* maintain physical contact with Ash to keep him upright and supported. But her hands were full and her concentration divided, and the signal was suffering for it.
Two choices. She saw them clearly, laid out like branches in a decision tree:
Take Ash and leave. Abandon the broadcast. Save his life and hope for another chance.
Broadcast now. Risk losing Ash — his weight against her, his glitch-dampened body a liability in the chaos that would follow.
She looked at him. He was watching her with those dark, drugged eyes, and she could see the thought forming on his face: *Leave me. Do the mission. I'm expendable.*
"No," she said out loud.
"What?"
"No. We're doing both."

She hooked her arms under his and hauled him upright. He groaned but didn't fight her. She dragged him to the core server, positioned him against the rack, angled his body so the server itself was taking most of his weight. He was propped up like a man who'd been left against a wall to catch his breath — present but supported.
She pressed the Key into his hand. Then covered his hand with hers.
"Feel that?" she asked.
"The pulse—"
"That's our signal. Hold onto it."
He closed his fingers around the Key. The pulse intensified — she could feel it through his skin, his blood, the glitch that was fighting through the suppressant and starting to burn.

She leaned her forehead against his. His breath was warm and ragged on her face. The proximity was overwhelming — his heartbeat against hers, his power wrestling with the drug, his presence solid and real and alive in a way it hadn't been in the cell.
"Together," she said.
She pushed.
And his power rose to meet hers.

It came from somewhere deep — beneath the suppressant, beneath the exhaustion, beneath the damage they'd done to him in that cell. Ash's glitch surged upward like a drowning person breaking the surface, gasping and desperate and *alive*. It crashed into her power, combined with it, multiplied.
The Signal Key amplified them both.

The broadcast SCREAMED through the facility's internal network. Every screen in Blackmoss lit up. Every speaker crackled with static. Every connected device — phones, tablets, diagnostic equipment, the Hounds' command systems — received a burst of data so intense it overloaded their buffer protocols.

But it hadn't reached the outside. The facility was on an isolated network. A firewall — thick, layered, military-grade — sat between Blackmoss and the city's telecommunications grid. She could feel it: a wall of encrypted silence that refused to let the signal pass.
She pushed against it. The firewall held.
The Signal Key pulsed. Ash's hand tightened under hers.
And Wren realized this was going to take everything she had.
